title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s are tricky, so I'd like to understand the full picture.

Can you walk me through what you're typically doing when the 403 hits? For example, are you loading the dashboard fresh after logging in, navigating to it from another page, or does it sometimes fail on a page you already have open (like after a refresh or returning from a different tab)? And when you say it started a couple days ago — did anything change around that time that you're aware of, like a deployment, a change in team roles, or even something as simple as switching browsers or networks?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It usually happens when I'm just loading the dashboard fresh — like I'll log in and go to the dashboard and get a 403. If I refresh the page it sometimes works on the next try. It doesn't seem to matter if I'm navigating from another page or loading it directly.

As for what changed — I was actually assigned a new role recently, the 'analyst' role. That was around the same time this started, now that I think about it. But my teammates who also got the analyst role are seeing the same thing, so I figured it wasn't just me. I assumed it was maybe network issues on our end since it's so inconsistent.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really helpful connection — the timing with the analyst role assignment is likely not a coincidence, especially since your teammates with the same role are seeing it too.

I'm curious about the pattern of when it works vs. when it doesn't. When you get the 403 and then refresh and it works — does it tend to stay working for a while after that, or can it flip back to 403 on the very next page load? And have you or your teammates noticed whether it's specific parts of the dashboard that fail, or is it the entire dashboard page that returns 403? I'm trying to understand whether this feels like a timing/loading issue (where the permissions sometimes aren't "ready" fast enough) or something more like a coin flip each time you load the page.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It's pretty much a coin flip every time. Like I can get a 403, refresh and it works, then refresh again and get another 403. It doesn't stay working once it starts working — each page load seems independent. And it's the entire dashboard page that returns the 403, not specific parts of it. It's not like some widgets load and others don't — the whole page either works or doesn't. I'd say it works maybe 2 out of 3 times and fails about 1 in 3, if I had to guess. That's why I thought it was a network thing.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks — the coin-flip pattern on every single load is really telling. That rules out session or network issues and points toward something inconsistent on the server side.

I want to make sure I understand the role situation correctly. Was the 'analyst' role something that already existed in TaskFlow and you were just assigned to it, or is it a newly created role that was set up around the same time you were assigned it? And before you had the analyst role, did you have a different role that let you access the dashboard without issues — and if so, do you still have that old role too, or was it replaced by the analyst one?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I think the analyst role is new — it was just recently added to TaskFlow, at least that's what our team lead told us. Before that I had the 'viewer' role, and I think it was replaced with the analyst role rather than being added on top of it. I didn't have any issues accessing the dashboard when I was a viewer.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 on dashboard for users with newly created 'analyst' role (likely inconsistent permission propagation)","problem":"Users assigned the newly created 'analyst' role (replacing 'viewer') receive 403 Forbidden errors on approximately 1 in 3 dashboard page loads. The failures are independent per request — refreshing can succeed, then fail again immediately. All users with the analyst role are affected. Users had no issues under the previous 'viewer' role.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The new 'analyst' role's dashboard permission is not consistently recognized across all serving instances. This is most likely caused by one of: (1) multiple app server instances behind a load balancer where the role-to-permission mapping was not propagated to all nodes (e.g., stale permission cache, incomplete config deploy), (2) a permissions cache with inconsistent TTL or replication lag, or (3) an eventual-consistency issue in the authorization data store where the analyst role's grant is not yet replicated to all read replicas.","reproduction_steps":["Create or use a user account with only the 'analyst' role (no 'viewer' or other roles)","Log in to TaskFlow","Navigate to the dashboard","Refresh the dashboard page 10–15 times in quick succession","Observe that approximately 1 in 3 loads returns 403 Forbidden"],"environment":"Affects all users with the newly created 'analyst' role. Multiple team members confirmed. Started approximately 2 days before report, coinciding with the introduction of the analyst role and role reassignment from 'viewer' to 'analyst'.","severity":"high","impact":"All users assigned the 'analyst' role are affected. Dashboard access is unreliable (~33% failure rate), blocking routine work. Likely affects the entire cohort of users migrated from 'viewer' to 'analyst'.","recommended_fix":"1. Check whether the 'analyst' role has an explicit dashboard access permission granted — compare its permission set against the 'viewer' role it replaced. 2. If permissions are correctly configured, investigate whether the authorization layer uses caching or multiple instances — check for replication lag or nodes with stale role/permission mappings. 3. Inspect server-side access logs correlated with 403 responses to identify whether specific app server instances or permission cache states correlate with failures. 4. As an immediate mitigation, consider re-granting 'viewer' role alongside 'analyst' for affected users while the root cause is fixed.","proposed_test_case":"Integration test: create a user with only the 'analyst' role, make 50 sequential authenticated requests to the dashboard endpoint, and assert that all 50 return 200. This validates consistent permission evaluation across requests regardless of which instance or cache state serves them.","information_gaps":["Whether the authorization system uses caching, replication, or multiple instances (server-side investigation needed)","Exact permission entries configured for the 'analyst' role vs. the 'viewer' role","Server-side logs showing which component returns the 403 (auth middleware, API gateway, application layer)"]}
